drafts.blog_title: When Should a Founder Hire a COO? (And Why It's Not When You Think)
drafts.draft_text:
The moment your team starts organizing their work around your schedule instead of around what the business needs, you've become the bottleneck.

Three months into my CEO role at D-tree, I realized people were saving up decisions for our weekly one-on-ones. Important decisions. Time-sensitive decisions.

But because I was the only one who could make them — or at least, that's what we all believed — they would sit and wait.

Meanwhile, I was ping-ponging between grant proposals and personnel issues and donor calls. Fifteen different contexts in a single day.

I was working harder than I ever had. And somehow, that was exactly the problem.

The instinct is to power through. To work longer hours, be more available, respond faster. But when you're the constraint in every workflow, working harder just creates more pressure in the system.

The real shift isn't about finding more hours in your day. It's about redesigning how decisions get made when you're not in the room.

Most founders wait until they're drowning to think about operational support. But the best time isn't when everything is broken — it's when you catch yourself becoming the thing that slows everyone else down.

How do you know when you've crossed that line?

#FractionalCOO #Leadership #Founders #OperationalExcellence #ScalingOrganizations

drafts.blog_title: What Is a Fractional COO? A CEO's Guide to Getting Unstuck
drafts.draft_text:
Most founders think they need more people. What they actually need is more clarity.

Three years into leading D-tree, I was working 70-hour weeks and still felt like I was drowning. We were hiring constantly, adding new programs, chasing new funding.

But growth felt chaotic instead of intentional.

The problem wasn't that I needed another full-time person on my leadership team. The problem was that too many decisions, processes, and institutional knowledge lived only in my head.

I needed someone who could help me get it out of my head and into systems the team could actually use.

That's what I learned a Fractional COO really does. Not manage your operations for you, but work alongside you to build the clarity and structure that makes everything else possible.

We spend the first few weeks diagnosing where the real bottlenecks are—and they're almost never where founders think they are. Then we build right-sized systems that support growth without over-engineering it.

The goal isn't to replace the founder's judgment. It's to free up their capacity to actually use it.

Are you working harder because you need more people, or because you need more clarity about how the work should flow?

#FractionalCOO #Leadership #Founders #OperationalExcellence #StartupOperations

drafts.blog_title: How to Get Your Leadership Team Aligned (Without Over-Engineering It)
drafts.draft_text:
The moment leadership teams start having the same conversations over and over, the instinct is to add more structure.

More frameworks. Longer meetings. Better agendas.

I learned the hard way that wasn't the problem.

We were three hours into a leadership retreat, still circling the same three priorities we'd discussed for months. Everyone was engaged. Everyone had opinions.

But when someone said "we need to focus on growth," we were all picturing different things.

One person meant new customers. Another meant new markets. A third was thinking about new products.

We were nodding in agreement about completely different strategies.

The breakthrough wasn't getting everyone to agree on everything. It was getting everyone to agree on the same definitions.

What does "growth" actually mean for us this quarter? What are we saying no to? Who makes the final call when we disagree?

Alignment isn't consensus. It's clarity.

How often is your leadership team having the same conversation because you haven't actually defined what you're talking about?

#Leadership #TeamAlignment #OperationalExcellence #FractionalCOO

drafts.blog_title: What Systems Does a 10-Person Team Actually Need?
drafts.draft_text:
Most teams try to solve chaos by adding more processes. That usually makes things worse.

When I was CEO at D-tree, we hit that inflection point where informal systems stopped working. Eight people had become fifteen, and suddenly no one knew what anyone else was doing.

My first instinct was to implement everything at once. Daily standups. Project boards. Status reports. Decision frameworks borrowed from much larger companies.

Six weeks later, people were spending more time updating systems than doing actual work.

So I stepped back and asked a different question: what's actually breaking down?

For us, it was simple. People didn't know what others were working on. Decisions were happening in side conversations. We kept rehashing the same discussions.

We didn't have a project management problem. We had a communication problem.

Once I saw that clearly, the solution became obvious. We needed three things: a weekly team sync, a shared project tracker that took 30 seconds to update, and a simple way to document decisions.

That's it.

The chaos didn't come from having too few systems. It came from not being intentional about the systems we did need.

What's actually breaking down in your team right now? Not what systems you think you should have—what's the real problem you're trying to solve?

#FractionalCOO #Leadership #OperationalExcellence #StartupOperations #SystemsThinking

drafts.blog_title: The Real Cost of Being the Founder Bottleneck
drafts.draft_text:
The moment you become essential to every decision is the moment you stop building a team. You start building dependents.

I learned this the hard way at D-tree. The feedback wasn't about my work quality or decision-making. It was simpler and more devastating: "You're working all the time and it's stressing the team out."

I thought constant availability was good leadership. Jump in on any project. Answer every question. Make myself useful.

What I didn't see was the pattern I was creating.

My team stopped making decisions because they knew I'd probably want to weigh in anyway. They stopped taking initiative because they weren't sure what I'd want them to do.

They started waiting instead of acting.

And then I worked harder to compensate for their hesitation, which made them hesitate more.

The real cost wasn't my time—though that was expensive enough. The real cost was that I was accidentally training capable people to be less capable.

When everything runs through you, nothing really runs without you.

What decisions are your team members waiting for you to make that they could handle themselves?

#Leadership #Founders #OperationalExcellence #FractionalCOO #GrowthLeadership
